**Liège Basket** was a [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") club from [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"). The club competed in the [BNXT League](/wiki/BNXT_League "BNXT League"). Founded in 1967, the team played at 5,600 seat [Country Hall Ethias Liège](/wiki/Country_Hall_Ethias_Li%C3%A8ge "Country Hall Ethias Liège").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The club was one of the traditional clubs in Belgian basketball, having played in the top flight division since 2001 for a period of 24 years. Liège Basket's accolades include one [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Basketball_Cup "Belgian Basketball Cup") (in 2004) and two [Belgian Supercups](/wiki/Belgian_Basketball_Supercup "Belgian Basketball Supercup") (in 2004 and 2009). The team was disestablished in 2024 after its main investor left the team, leaving insufficient funds to continue the club.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li%C3%A8ge_Basket&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The club was founded in [Fléron](/wiki/Fl%C3%A9ron "Fléron") as the Fléron Basket Club in 1967. After being an amateur league for its first years, the team hired its first professional coach in 1975. In 1977 and 1981, the Fléron promoted to the first regional league. In 1983, the club made its debut on the national level as it promoted to the Fourth National Division. In 2000, the club decided to merge with BC Hannut and Essor Hannut, relocating to the city of [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège").

After the move to Lìege, the club quickly promoted to the [First National League](/wiki/Pro_Basketball_League "Pro Basketball League"). In 2001, the club's debut in Europe was made after it qualified as 7th in the previous season. In the [2001–02 FIBA Korać Cup](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_FIBA_Kora%C4%87_Cup "2001–02 FIBA Korać Cup"), the team lost to French side [Racing Paris](/wiki/Paris_Racing_Basket "Paris Racing Basket") in the first round. Three years later, in 2004, the team wins its first silverware when it captures the [Belgian Basketball Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Basketball_Cup "Belgian Basketball Cup") for the first time.

In the 2008–09 season, the team had its best European campaign when it reached the Top 16 of the [2008–09 FIBA EuroChallenge](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_FIBA_EuroChallenge "2008–09 FIBA EuroChallenge"). In 2010, Liège played in the championship playoffs for the first time, but loses to [Spirou Charleroi](/wiki/Spirou_Charleroi "Spirou Charleroi").

Since the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_BNXT_League "2021–22 BNXT League"), Liège plays in the [BNXT League](/wiki/BNXT_League "BNXT League"), in which the national leagues of Belgium and the Netherlands have been merged.[[2]](#cite_note-2) After the 2020s were characterised mainly by financial problems for Liège, the club was purchased by an American investors group in 2022. The Mickael Sports Group, owned by Ernie Cambo, purchased all shares in the team.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Following the acquisition, head coach [Lionel Bosco](/wiki/Lionel_Bosco "Lionel Bosco") was sacked and replaced by [Brad Greenberg](/wiki/Brad_Greenberg "Brad Greenberg").

On October 3, the club announced that they will be boycotting the away-game against [Antwerp Giants](/wiki/Antwerp_Giants "Antwerp Giants") on October 28 because of "partisan" refereeing in away-games, also saying that "Belgian players get better calls then foreigners. The [BNXT League](/wiki/BNXT_League "BNXT League") responded by saying that if they actually pull through, Liège will be refused access to the playoffs on top of a €2500 fine. The League put the deadline for the decision on October 10. On October 9, they announced that the will play against the [Antwerp Giants](/wiki/Antwerp_Giants "Antwerp Giants").

Following the 2023–24 season, owner Ernie Cambo announced the stop of funding of the basketball team, citing the lack of support from the municapility. He added that the slow support from sponsors, as well as the upcoming reconstruction of the Country Hall led to the decision. As a result, the team was later disestablished, ending a 58-year history of the club.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
